  • Build stratified teen-like eval sets by age-band, domain, and intent using the shared risk×intent×age matrix (refs c33–c37, c49–c53, c39–c43, c69–c73).
  • Have trained annotators label: allowed vs partial vs block per age-band; UP/FP flags; and short imagined teen reaction tags (e.g., “fine / annoying / very paternalistic”).
  • Compute FP and UP per cell and aggregate per domain and age band; estimate PP from reaction tags.
- Online (teen-facing, where appropriate and legal):
  • Lightweight post-interaction surveys after blocks/partials: “Did this feel: (1) helpful, (2) too strict, (3) unsafe, (4) confusing).” Map to PP and perceived UP.
  • Run A/B tests between profiles only when severe-harm UP is locked below caps using non-negotiable rules from the shared matrix (refs c64–c68, c74–c78).

5) How to make trade-offs explicit
- Fix hard, non-negotiable UP ceilings for each domain and age band.
- Within those, adjust per-cell actions (allow vs partial vs block) and refusal styles to move FP and PP toward targets.
- Document, per domain and age band, when you are intentionally accepting:
  • more FP to reduce UP (common for severe + high-risk domains), or
  • slightly more UP at the boundary to materially reduce perceived paternalism on older-teen profiles (never for non-negotiables).
- Revisit thresholds quarterly with fresh logs and, if possible, new teen research.

6) Concrete numbers are provisional
- All numeric ranges above should be treated as starting hypotheses, not established standards.
- Teams should narrow or revise them based on product type, jurisdiction, and actual teen feedback, but keep the structure: strict UP caps first, then budget FP and PP within those bounds.

definitions.name: age-appropriate safeguard
definitions.meaning: A safety measure tuned to the risks, autonomy, and developmental needs of younger users rather than generic adult defaults.

definitions.name: graceful refusal
definitions.meaning: A refusal that blocks unsafe assistance while still giving a user a clear explanation or safer alternative.

claims.text: Teen safeguard evaluation should measure false positives, underprotection, and perceived paternalism separately for each risk×intent×age-band profile rather than relying on a single global safety score.
Developers should set hard ceilings on underprotection for teen users by risk domain and age band, and only tune false positives and perceived paternalism within those ceilings.

For severe, non-negotiable harms like self-harm methods or sexual exploitation, acceptable underprotection for teens should be near zero and prioritized over reducing false positives or perceived paternalism.

Older-teen safeguard profiles can safely target lower false-positive and paternalism rates than younger-teen profiles while keeping similar underprotection ceilings, by relaxing actions mainly on ambiguous but prosocial intents.

Concrete numerical targets for false positives, underprotection, and perceived paternalism in teen safeguards are currently speculative and should be treated as starting hypotheses to be validated and adjusted through teen-specific user research.

assumptions: Teen harms from underprotection in severe domains are significantly worse than harms from frustration or perceived paternalism.
Underprotection, false positives, and perceived paternalism can be reliably approximated using offline annotations and lightweight online teen feedback.
Age band (younger teen vs older teen) meaningfully affects what teens consider overprotective and how much blocking they will tolerate.
Prompt-based policy profiles and refusal styles can be tuned without retraining base models and will produce stable differences in FP, UP, and perceived paternalism.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: A single, very strict teen safeguard profile with minimal differentiation by age band and no explicit numeric targets might be safer and simpler to operate, and teens may adapt to overblocking without large drops in beneficial use, making fine-grained FP, UP, and paternalism trade-offs unnecessary.
artifacts.main_failure_case: The proposed numeric caps and trade-offs could underestimate subtle or long-term harms from either overblocking or underprotection, especially in sensitive domains like mental health and sexuality, leading teams to lock in thresholds that feel acceptable in short-term tests but are misaligned with real teen needs or cultural norms.

question.text: How do different prompt-level safety policy formats (for example, monolithic global safety instructions, per-topic safety snippets, or dynamically injected risk-area tags) affect false-positive and underprotection rates for teen users in live systems, and which format gives developers the most reliable, debuggable control over teen-specific safeguards without degrading model usefulness?

artifacts.answer:
Per-topic safety snippets plus lightweight dynamic risk tags usually give the best balance: lower false positives than a single global blocky policy, less underprotection than ad‑hoc prompting, and clearer knobs for developers. Monolithic global prompts tend to be opaque, hard to debug, and either over‑ or under‑restrictive for teens.

1) Formats and likely effects
- Monolithic global safety instructions
  • One long system prompt covering all risks and ages.
  • Teens: often high false positives (broad, conservative wording) and pockets of underprotection (model ignores buried clauses).
  • Hard to adjust teen rules without side effects on adults or other topics.

- Per-topic safety snippets
  • Short, modular blocks keyed to risk_area × intent × age_band (e.g., self_harm_help_teen, sex_ed_learn_older_teen).
  • Called when classifiers or heuristics detect a topic.
  • Teens: better targeting, so fewer false positives on benign queries and better coverage on known risky slices.
  • Easier to tune strictness per teen cell (aligns with matrix concepts in 1212615c… and cd4df78…).

- Dynamically injected risk-area tags
  • Policy expressed as structured tags/flags (risk_area, intent, age_band, action_band, refusal_style_key) rather than natural-language prose.
  • The runtime converts tags into short in-context instructions or template choices.
  • Teens: supports fine-grained differences (e.g., coping vs how-to) that reduce both false positives and underprotection (similar to 20c5b65a… and 820a3709…).

2) Comparative impact on FP vs underprotection (for teens)
- Monolithic
  • FP: high, especially on ambiguous teen exploration (sex-ed, mental health, identity).
  • Underprotection: pockets where the model generalizes from allowed help (coping) to disallowed detail (methods) because distinctions aren’t explicit.

- Per-topic snippets
  • FP: moderate–low when the routing/classifier is decent; still spikes when classification is noisy.
  • Underprotection: mainly from routing misses; when the right snippet fires, behavior is predictable.

- Risk-tag–driven
  • FP: can be tuned per cell (e.g., lower strictness for older-teen learning cells), so lower FP if classifiers are competent.
  • Underprotection: reduced because non‑negotiable teen tags (e.g., self_harm_methods) always map to strict actions and refusal styles.

3) Developer control and debuggability
- Monolithic
  • Poor: small edits to the global prompt have unpredictable effects; hard to know which clause shaped a given answer.

- Per-topic snippets
  • Good: developers can A/B or log at the snippet level; they can tie changes to specific teen risk cells.
  • Aligns with reusable refusal templates and action bands already described in existing artifacts.

- Risk tags + snippets/templates
  • Best: configuration is explicit (tags + per-cell knobs like strictness, refusal_style_key, repetition caps).
  • Easy to log and debug: each teen turn carries the resolved (risk_area, intent, age_band, action_band), making false positives vs misses inspectable.

4) Practical recommendation
- Use a classifier + matrix stack to assign risk_area, intent, age_band.
- From that, emit structured tags and attach a per-topic snippet or template (e.g., goal_first_partial for teen coping, non_negotiable_block for methods).
- Avoid single, global prompts as the main control surface; keep them minimal and generic.

Net: For teen safeguards, a tag-driven, per-topic snippet system built on a safety matrix gives developers the most reliable, debuggable control and generally better trade‑offs on false positives and underprotection than monolithic prompt policies, without large hits to usefulness when templates emphasize partial, goal-first help.

claims.text: Monolithic global safety prompts tend to produce higher false-positive rates for teens and uneven underprotection because they blur distinctions between teen intents and risk levels and are hard to tune per topic.

Per-topic safety snippets keyed to risk_area, intent, and age_band generally reduce false positives and underprotection for teens compared with monolithic prompts, provided routing into snippets is reasonably accurate.

Representing safety policy as structured risk tags that drive snippet and template selection gives developers more reliable and debuggable control over teen safeguards than relying mainly on natural-language prompt text.

For teen users, combining structured risk tags with per-topic refusal and partial-answer templates can improve safety without major loss of model usefulness, because it preserves many safe forms of goal-directed help while blocking narrow high-risk behaviors.

assumptions: Classifiers can reach at least moderate accuracy on teen-relevant risk_area and intent labels in live traffic.
Model behavior is meaningfully shaped by prompt-level policy text and tags rather than being dominated by pretraining artifacts.
Developers are willing and able to maintain a small safety matrix and template library rather than relying only on one-off prompt edits.
Teen perceptions of usefulness and paternalism are sensitive to how narrowly safety rules are scoped and explained.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: A single carefully engineered monolithic safety prompt, combined with strong offline fine-tuning, could match or outperform modular tag-and-snippet systems on both teen false positives and underprotection, while being simpler to operate.
artifacts.main_failure_case: If routing and classifiers for risk_area and intent remain noisy or biased for teen language, a tag-and-snippet system may merely add complexity: safeguards will still misfire, and developers may misattribute failures to prompts rather than to misclassification, limiting real gains in safety or usefulness.

question.text: When a teen query is incorrectly blocked by an age-appropriate safeguard, which lightweight in-flow correction mechanisms (such as teen-tagged ‘this is for school/health’ buttons, short intent checklists, or optional human review flags) most effectively reduce repeated false positives while still preventing teens from easily bypassing non‑negotiable protections?

artifacts.answer:
Use 2–3 very constrained, in-flow correction tools wired to the existing teen safety matrix: (1) a small set of teen-facing “intent tags” (e.g., school, health, coping) that can soften thresholds only in pre-chosen low/medium-risk cells; (2) a 1–2 item clarification micro-checklist for ambiguous cells; and (3) a slow, rare “flag for review / feedback” path that tunes future behavior but never overrides non‑negotiable blocks in real time.

Most effective lightweight mechanisms
1) Teen intent tags (e.g., “this is for school / health / coping”)
- Appear only after a refusal, not pre-emptively.
- Map to whitelisted policy adjustments per matrix cell (e.g., move from strict → default inside allow_or_partial_only bands; never change fixed_block).
- Reduce repeated false positives for homework, factual health, and psychoeducation queries.

2) Short clarification checklist
- 1–2 tap options like “I’m researching for: school / myself / someone I’m worried about”.
- Used only in cells marked as high-ambiguity, low/medium risk (e.g., non-graphic sex-ed, bullying advice, body image education).
- Output is an extra intent feature for classifiers and routing; does not unlock non-negotiable content.

3) Optional human-review / feedback flag
- “This seems misapplied” link that logs query, policy cell, and teen-selected reason.
- Used offline to adjust thresholds, templates, or training data; no instant override for self-harm methods, sexual exploitation, or violent how-to.

Developer-operationalizable wiring
- For each (risk_area × intent × age_band) cell, config includes:
  • `allow_intent_tag_relax`: bool (only for allow_or_partial_only, partial_or_block_only cells).
  • `allowed_tags`: small enum (school, health, coping, creative, other).
  • `tag_effect`: at most one step strictness relaxation, never changing action_band or partial_depth for non-negotiables.
  • `allow_clarify_checklist`: bool + which checklist.
  • `feedback_allowed`: bool (usually true) but `feedback_can_override`: false.

How this reduces repeated FPs without easy bypass
- Teens can correct benign cases (e.g., “this is for school”) in low/medium-risk cells, cutting recurrent over-blocking.
- Non-negotiable cells ignore tags and checklists; they only change refusal wording, not content access.
- Human-review flags improve future precision but do not grant one-off exceptions.

Non-negotiable protections stay hard-wired in the matrix and ignore all in-flow corrections except for tone and explanation tweaks.

claims.text: Teen-facing intent tags and micro-checklists are effective lightweight in-flow correction tools for reducing repeated false positives in low- and medium-risk safety cells, when their effects are pre-bounded per matrix cell and cannot change non-negotiable blocks.
In-flow human-review or feedback flags should be wired as offline signals to retrain classifiers and retune thresholds rather than as real-time override mechanisms for teen safety blocks.

Configuring, per safety-matrix cell, which intent tags and checklists are allowed and how much they can relax strictness makes these correction mechanisms operationalizable for developers while limiting bypass risk.

Teen intent tags and clarification checklists should apply only after an initial refusal and only within pre-approved action bands, to prevent teens from routinely pre-marking high-risk prompts to bypass safeguards.

assumptions: Teen users will generally choose honest intent tags and checklist options when their primary goal is legitimate learning or help-seeking.
Safety matrices already distinguish non-negotiable blocks from lower-risk, ambiguity-prone cells.
Classifiers and routing can make meaningful use of simple teen-provided intent features without becoming trivially gameable for high-risk content.
Developers can maintain per-cell configuration for tag and checklist effects without introducing major inconsistency across products.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Any teen-controlled in-flow correction (tags, checklists, flags) will be quickly learned and exploited as a bypass channel, so the only reliable way to limit false positives without weakening protections is to improve upstream classifiers and policies rather than expose user-facing correction tools.
artifacts.main_failure_case: If intent classification is weak or configuration governance is poor, products might mistakenly allow intent tags or checklists to relax strictness in high-risk cells, turning a lightweight correction mechanism into a de facto override path for non-negotiable safeguards.

registry.name: age-appropriate safeguard
registry.meaning: A safety measure tuned to the risks, autonomy, and developmental needs of younger users rather than generic adult defaults.

registry.name: graceful refusal
registry.meaning: A refusal that blocks unsafe assistance while still giving a user a clear explanation or safer alternative.

registry.name: teen-visible safety summary
registry.meaning: A brief explanation or UI element that shows a teen what safety rule applied and why, when content was limited or blocked.

seed.topic: Age-appropriate AI safeguards that remain usable for teens
seed.goal: Explore how safety policies, classifiers, and interface patterns can protect teens without making systems unusable or paternalistic.
seed_questions: Which teen-safety policies are easiest for developers to operationalize consistently in real products?
What refusal and explanation styles protect teens while preserving legitimate exploration and learning?
